```{r peer_review_survey_timing, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Quick histogram of gestational age at survey
ggplot(survey_timing, aes(x = gestage_weeks)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 210 / 7, linetype = "dashed", color = "red",
             linewidth = 0.8) +   # DLNM eligibility cutoff (30 weeks)
  geom_vline(xintercept = 135 / 7, linetype = "dotted", color = "darkgreen",
             linewidth = 0.8) +   # start of DLNM-sensitive window
  annotate("text", x = 210/7 + 0.4, y = Inf, vjust = 1.5,
           label = "Eligibility\ncutoff (30 wk)", size = 3, color = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = 135/7 - 0.4, y = Inf, vjust = 1.5, hjust = 1,
           label = "Day 135\n(sensitive\nwindow start)", size = 3, color = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    x = "Gestational age at survey (weeks)",
    y = "Number of participants",
    title = "Distribution of gestational age at survey — DLNM analytical sample",
    caption = "Red dashed line = DLNM eligibility cutoff (≥30 weeks). Green dotted line = start of DLNM-identified sensitive window (day 135)."
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12)

```

---

## 1b. Missing data profile
*Reviewer 1: requested explicit statement of what variables had missingness, how much, whether complete-case analysis was used, and whether excluded participants differed from included ones.*

```{r peer_review_missing_data, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# ── MISSING DATA PROFILE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Start from the full crisis_recode dataset (all surveyed participants before
# any analysis-specific filtering) to characterise missingness in the source data.
# Then show what happens at each filtering step to arrive at crisis_dlnm_eligible.

cat("=== Step 1: Full crisis_recode sample (all CRiSYS surveys) ===\n")
cat("  N =", nrow(crisis_recode), "\n\n")


# Missingness in analysis variables, in the full dataset
cat("=== Missingness in key analysis variables (full crisis_recode sample) ===\n")
crisis_recode %>%
  summarise(
    n_total            = n(),
    miss_outcome       = sum(is.na(sum_nds_category)),
    miss_bmi           = sum(is.na(bmi)),
    miss_age           = sum(is.na(age)),
    miss_wealthindex   = sum(is.na(wealthindex)),
    miss_pregchn       = sum(is.na(pregchn)),
    miss_married       = sum(is.na(married)),
    miss_medlev        = sum(is.na(medlev)),
    miss_gestage       = sum(is.na(gestage_days)),
    miss_date_delivery = sum(is.na(datdeliv))
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_missing") %>%
  mutate(
    pct_missing = round(100 * n_missing / crisis_recode %>% nrow(), 1),
    variable = str_remove(variable, "^miss_") %>%
      dplyr::recode(
        "n_total"          = "N total",
        "outcome"          = "Sum NDS category (outcome)",
        "bmi"              = "BMI",
        "age"              = "Age",
        "wealthindex"      = "Wealth index",
        "pregchn"          = "Parity",
        "married"          = "Marital status",
        "medlev"           = "Education level",
        "gestage"          = "Gestational age",
        "date_delivery"    = "Delivery date"
      )
  ) %>%
  print(n = 20)


# Show the filtering steps to reach the analytical sample
cat("\n=== Filtering steps from full sample to DLNM analytical sample ===\n")

n_full <- nrow(crisis_recode)

# Step 1: filter to gestage >= 210 (DLNM eligibility)
n_after_gestage <- crisis_recode %>%
  filter(gestage_at_survey >= 210) %>%
  nrow()

# Step 2: additionally filter out missing BMI (only other exclusion in main model)
n_after_bmi <- crisis_recode %>%
  filter(gestage_at_survey >= 210, !is.na(bmi)) %>%
  nrow()

# Step 3: additionally require non-missing WBGT at day_0
# (this mirrors the filter(!is.na(day_0)) step in the DLNM setup)
# Use the temperature_lags_wide object if it exists in the environment;
# otherwise report n_after_bmi as the final analytic N.
n_dlnm_eligible <- nrow(crisis_dlnm_eligible)

tibble(
  Step = c(
    "1. Full CRiSYS survey sample",
    "2. Surveyed at ≥210 gestational days (DLNM eligibility cutoff)",
    "3. Non-missing BMI (required covariate)",
    "4. DLNM analytical sample (crisis_dlnm_eligible)"
  ),
  N = c(n_full, n_after_gestage, n_after_bmi, n_dlnm_eligible),
  N_excluded = c(NA, n_full - n_after_gestage,
                 n_after_gestage - n_after_bmi,
                 n_after_bmi - n_dlnm_eligible)
) %>%
  print()


cat("\n=== Missingness in analysis covariates — DLNM analytical sample only ===\n")
crisis_dlnm_eligible %>%
  summarise(
    n_total          = n(),
    miss_bmi         = sum(is.na(bmi)),
    miss_age         = sum(is.na(age)),
    miss_wealthindex = sum(is.na(wealthindex)),
    miss_pregchn     = sum(is.na(pregchn)),
    miss_married     = sum(is.na(married)),
    miss_medlev      = sum(is.na(medlev)),
    miss_outcome     = sum(is.na(sum_nds_category))
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_missing") %>%
  mutate(pct_missing = round(100 * n_missing / crisis_dlnm_eligible %>% nrow(), 1)) %>%
  print()

```

---

## 1c. ERA5 spatial structure
*Reviewer 2, Comment 3: requested a clear description of how many distinct ERA5 exposure-assignment locations participants were drawn from. ERA5 has ~25 km resolution; communities within the same grid cell share identical daily exposure histories, reducing the effective spatial sample size below N = 312.*

```{r peer_review_era5_structure, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# ── ERA5 SPATIAL STRUCTURE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# ERA5 grid resolution: 0.25 degrees (~25 km).
# Each community centroid was snapped to the nearest ERA5 grid point for
# exposure extraction. Communities within the same grid cell therefore share
# exactly the same daily WBGT time series.

# Load community centroid coordinates
centroids <- read_csv(
  here("data", "Community Centroids GRAPHS- CommunityCentroids_MM_DM_revised_10312023.csv")
)

# Inspect column names to confirm lat/lon field names
cat("=== Column names in community centroid file ===\n")
names(centroids) %>% print()
head(centroids)

```

```{r peer_review_era5_grid_cells, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# NOTE: adjust 'lat_col' and 'lon_col' below to match the actual column names
# printed above (e.g. "lat", "latitude", "LAT", "Y", etc.)
lat_col <- "lat"   # ← change if needed after running the chunk above
lon_col <- "lon"   # ← change if needed

# Snap each community centroid to the nearest ERA5 0.25-degree grid cell
# by rounding to the nearest 0.25 degree.
round_to_025 <- function(x) round(x / 0.25) * 0.25

era5_assignment <- centroids %>%
  rename(lat = all_of(lat_col), lon = all_of(lon_col)) %>%
  mutate(
    era5_lat = round_to_025(lat),
    era5_lon = round_to_025(lon),
    era5_cell = paste0(era5_lat, "_", era5_lon)   # unique cell identifier
  )

# How many distinct ERA5 grid cells cover this sample?
cat("=== ERA5 grid cell summary ===\n")
era5_assignment %>%
  summarise(
    n_communities  = n_distinct(era5_assignment %>% pull(1)),  # col 1 = community name
    n_era5_cells   = n_distinct(era5_cell)
  ) %>%
  print()

# Which communities share a grid cell?
# Using the confirmed "community" column from the centroid file (not col 1 = WKT).
cat("\n=== Communities per ERA5 grid cell ===\n")
era5_assignment %>%
  group_by(era5_cell, era5_lat, era5_lon) %>%
  summarise(
    n_communities = n(),
    communities   = paste(sort(community), collapse = ", "),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_communities)) %>%
  print(n = 30)


# How many analytical participants are assigned to each ERA5 cell?
# Join centroid "community" column to participant "vname" column.
# Both should hold the community name, but the ERA5 data loading step already
# recoded "AKORA/AT" → "AKORA" in vname; the centroid file may still have the
# original name. The unmatched check below will flag any remaining mismatches.
participants_per_cell <- crisis_dlnm_eligible %>%
  left_join(
    era5_assignment %>% select(community, era5_cell),
    by = c("vname" = "community")
  ) %>%
  group_by(era5_cell) %>%
  summarise(n_participants = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(n_participants))

# Diagnose any join failures (NA era5_cell = vname didn't match centroid file)
unmatched <- crisis_dlnm_eligible %>%
  left_join(era5_assignment %>% select(community, era5_cell),
            by = c("vname" = "community")) %>%
  filter(is.na(era5_cell)) %>%
  distinct(vname)

if (nrow(unmatched) > 0) {
  cat("\n⚠ Unmatched vname values (fix spelling to complete the join):\n")
  print(unmatched)
  cat("\nAvailable community names in centroid file:\n")
  print(sort(era5_assignment$community))
} else {
  cat("\n✓ All participant vname values matched the centroid file.\n")
}

cat("\n=== Participants per ERA5 grid cell (analytical sample) ===\n")
print(participants_per_cell)

cat("\nTotal analytical participants:", sum(participants_per_cell$n_participants, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
cat("Distinct ERA5 cells:          ", participants_per_cell %>% filter(!is.na(era5_cell)) %>% nrow(), "\n")
cat("Mean participants per cell:   ",
    round(mean(participants_per_cell$n_participants[!is.na(participants_per_cell$era5_cell)]), 1), "\n")
cat("Range:                        ",
    min(participants_per_cell$n_participants[!is.na(participants_per_cell$era5_cell)]), "–",
    max(participants_per_cell$n_participants[!is.na(participants_per_cell$era5_cell)]), "\n")

```

```{r, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
print("Summary of Sum NDS data")
summary(crisis_dlnm_eligible$sum_nds)
```



# ============================================================================
# PEER REVIEW RESPONSE - PHASE 2a: SEASONAL ADJUSTMENT SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================
#
# WHAT THIS ADDRESSES:
#   - Referee 1's main concern: our primary models adjust for
#     `season_delivery` (wet vs. dry season AT DELIVERY), which may be
#     collinear with the WBGT exposure windows themselves (overadjustment),
#     and may not be the most meaningful representation of calendar-time
#     confounding.
#   - Referee 2, Comment 2: requests (a) an alternative seasonal/calendar
#     adjustment (e.g., calendar month or sin/cos terms) and (b) a
#     conception-month sensitivity analysis.
#
# WHAT THIS CODE DOES:
#   Re-fits the 5 primary trimester-average models (the `models` list
#   created above, using `stress_data_with_temp`) three times, swapping
#   out ONLY the seasonal adjustment term each time:
#     1) season_delivery               -- original (wet/dry at delivery)
#     2) month_conception               -- calendar month of conception
#                                           (addresses the "conception-month
#                                           sensitivity analysis" request)
#     3) sin/cos of conception day-of-year -- smooth, 2-df harmonic term
#                                           representing the annual cycle,
#                                           which avoids the hard wet/dry
#                                           cutoff and is less likely to be
#                                           collinear with WBGT
#
#   All other covariates (age, bmi, wealthindex, pregchn, married_bin,
#   medlev_bin) are held identical to the primary models so the WBGT
#   exposure estimates are directly comparable across specs.
#
#   A small VIF (variance inflation factor) check is also included to
#   directly address the "season vs. temperature collinearity" concern.
# ============================================================================

```{r peer_review_seasonal_sensitivity}

# ---- 1) Create calendar-time covariates on stress_data_with_temp ---------
# (stress_data_with_temp was created earlier via calc_custom_temp_avg() and
#  already contains date_conception for every participant)

stress_data_with_temp <- stress_data_with_temp %>%
  mutate(
    # Calendar month of CONCEPTION (Jan-Dec factor) -- this is the
    # "conception-month sensitivity analysis" Referee 2 requested
    month_conception = factor(lubridate::month(date_conception, label = TRUE)),

    # Day-of-year of conception (1-366), used to build a smooth seasonal term
    doy_conception = lubridate::yday(date_conception),

    # Harmonic (sin/cos) pair representing the annual seasonal cycle as a
    # smooth continuous function, rather than a hard wet/dry cutoff.
    # Using a single sin/cos pair = 1 annual cycle (period = 365.25 days)
    sin_doy_conception = sin(2 * pi * doy_conception / 365.25),
    cos_doy_conception = cos(2 * pi * doy_conception / 365.25)
  )


# ---- 2) Define the exposure windows (same order as `models`, above) ------
exposure_vars <- c(
  "avg_temp_pre_conception",
  "avg_temp_first_trimester",
  "avg_temp_second_trimester",
  "avg_temp_third_trimester",
  "avg_temp_entire_pregnancy"
)

window_names <- names(models)  # "Pre-Conception", "First Trimester", ...


# ---- 3) Define the alternative seasonal/calendar-time adjustment specs ---
# Each entry is the piece of the model formula that replaces `season_delivery`
season_specs <- list(
  "No seasonal adjustment"            = NULL,
  "Season at delivery (original)"     = "season_delivery",
  "Calendar month of conception"      = "month_conception",
  "Sin/cos of conception day-of-year" = "sin_doy_conception + cos_doy_conception"
)


# ---- 4) Helper: refit one (exposure window x seasonal spec) combination ---
# Identical to the primary model specification, except the seasonal term
# is swapped out via `season_term`
fit_seasonal_sensitivity <- function(exposure_var, season_term) {
  # season_term is NULL for the "no seasonal adjustment" spec
  season_part <- if (is.null(season_term)) "" else paste0(" + ", season_term)
  f <- as.formula(
    paste0(
      "sum_nds_category ~ ", exposure_var,
      " + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn",
      season_part,
      " + married_bin + medlev_bin"
    )
  )

  polr(f, data = stress_data_with_temp, method = "logistic",
       na.action = na.omit, Hess = TRUE)
}


# ---- 5) Fit all (window x seasonal spec) combinations and pull out the ---
#         WBGT exposure term's OR, 95% CI, p-value, and N
seasonal_sensitivity_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(seq_along(exposure_vars), function(i) {

  exposure_var <- exposure_vars[i]
  window_name  <- window_names[i]

  purrr::imap_dfr(season_specs, function(season_term, spec_name) {

    fit <- fit_seasonal_sensitivity(exposure_var, season_term)

    # re-use the tidy_polr() helper defined earlier in this file
    tidy_polr(fit) %>%
      filter(term == exposure_var) %>%
      transmute(
        Window = window_name,
        `Seasonal adjustment` = spec_name,
        OR  = exp(estimate),
        lo  = exp(estimate - 1.96 * std.error),
        hi  = exp(estimate + 1.96 * std.error),
        `OR (95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", OR, lo, hi),
        `P-value` = p.value,
        N = nrow(model.frame(fit))
      )
  })
})

seasonal_sensitivity_tbl


# ---- 6) Collinearity check: VIF for WBGT exposure under each seasonal ----
#         spec (Referee 1's overadjustment / collinearity concern)
# We check the "Entire Pregnancy" window as the representative case, since
# it spans the widest exposure period and is most likely to overlap with
# season. A VIF > ~5 for the WBGT term would indicate problematic
# collinearity with the seasonal adjustment. GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) > ~2.2 would indicate concern



library(car)  # for vif()

vif_check_tbl <- purrr::imap_dfr(season_specs, function(season_term, spec_name) {

  fit <- fit_seasonal_sensitivity("avg_temp_entire_pregnancy", season_term)

  # vif() on a polr object returns a named vector (or matrix for factors
  # with >1 df); GVIF^(1/(2*df)) column gives a df-adjusted, comparable VIF
  v <- car::vif(fit)

  # Handle both vector (no multi-df terms) and matrix (factor terms) output
  if (is.matrix(v)) {
    vif_exposure <- v["avg_temp_entire_pregnancy", "GVIF^(1/(2*Df))"]
  } else {
    vif_exposure <- sqrt(v["avg_temp_entire_pregnancy"])  # put on same scale as GVIF^(1/(2*Df))
  }

  tibble(
    `Seasonal adjustment` = spec_name,
    `VIF (WBGT, entire pregnancy)` = round(vif_exposure, 2)
  )
})

vif_check_tbl


# ---- 7) Build a comparison gt table for the supplement --------------------
seasonal_sensitivity_table_save <- seasonal_sensitivity_tbl %>%
  mutate(
    Window = factor(Window, levels = window_names),
    `Seasonal adjustment` = factor(`Seasonal adjustment`, levels = names(season_specs)),
    `P-value` = format_p_lancet(`P-value`)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Window, `Seasonal adjustment`) %>%
  select(Window, `Seasonal adjustment`, `OR (95% CI)`, `P-value`, N) %>%
  gt(groupname_col = "Window") %>%
  cols_align("center") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Sensitivity of WBGT-stress associations to seasonal/calendar-time adjustment",
    subtitle = "Comparing season-at-delivery (original) vs. calendar month of conception vs. sin/cos seasonal terms"
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size  = px(12),
    data_row.padding = px(4),
    heading.align    = "left"
  )

seasonal_sensitivity_table_save


# ---- 8) Save outputs -------------------------------------------------------
gtsave(
  data = seasonal_sensitivity_table_save,
  filename = here("outputs", "Supplemental_Seasonal_Sensitivity_Table_trimester.rtf")
)

gtsave(
  seasonal_sensitivity_table_save,
  here("outputs", "seasonal_sensitivity_table_trimester.png"),
  vwidth = 1800, vheight = 1400, expand = 5
)

gtsave(
  seasonal_sensitivity_table_save,
  here("outputs", "seasonal_sensitivity_table_trimester.pdf")
)
```

**How to interpret this for the response letter:**

- If the WBGT odds ratios (and their significance) are **similar across all
  three seasonal adjustment specs**, this directly rebuts the overadjustment
  /collinearity concern: the WBGT-stress association is not an artifact of
  how season is coded, and is robust to using conception-month or a smooth
  seasonal cycle instead of season-at-delivery.
- The **VIF table** (Step 6) quantifies collinearity directly -- a low VIF
  (e.g., < 5, ideally close to 1-2) for the WBGT term under all three specs
  shows that adjusting for season does not substantially inflate the
  variance of the WBGT estimate, addressing Referee 1's main concern head-on.
- If results for the **calendar month of conception** spec are materially
  different, this also serves as the "conception-month sensitivity analysis"
  Referee 2 requested in Comment 2.


# ============================================================================
# PEER REVIEW RESPONSE - PHASE 2a (CONTINUED): SEASONAL ADJUSTMENT
# SENSITIVITY FOR THE PRIMARY DLNM MODEL (Figures 1 & 2)
# ============================================================================
#
# WHY THIS IS NEEDED IN ADDITION TO THE ANALYSIS ABOVE:
#   The sensitivity analysis above only re-fit the trimester-AVERAGE models
#   (the `models` list). It did NOT touch the primary DLNM model
#   (`model_polr`, built on `cb` + `temperature_lags_wide`), which is the
#   basis for Figures 1 & 2 and the weekly cumulative-effect tables -- i.e.
#   the headline result of the paper.
#
#   Referee 2's Comment 2, point (1) explicitly asks for adjustment for
#   "the specific calendar month or sin/cos terms FOR THE RELEVANT EXPOSURE
#   PERIOD, not just the season of delivery." For the DLNM, the relevant
#   exposure period is the full conception-aligned window (-30 to +210
#   days), so `season_delivery` (a single wet/dry label tied to the
#   DELIVERY date, which can be up to ~8 months after conception) is an
#   even weaker match here than for the trimester models. `date_conception`
#   is the natural anchor for this whole window, making month-of-conception
#   / sin-cos-of-conception-day exactly the right covariates to test.
#
# WHAT THIS CODE DOES:
#   IMPORTANT: by this point in the script, the global objects `cb`,
#   `model_polr`, `temperature_lags_wide`, `temp_data_matrix`, and
#   `temp_median` have all been OVERWRITTEN by later DLNM blocks (the last
#   one builds a model for `wbgt_delta`, not max WBGT!). To avoid silently
#   reusing the wrong model/crossbasis, Step 1 below REBUILDS the max-WBGT
#   DLNM inputs from scratch, using distinct `*_fig1` names, with the exact
#   same pipeline as the original Figure 1 block (lines ~143-215).
#
#   We then re-fit this max-WBGT model three times, swapping out only the
#   seasonal term (same 3 specs as above). For each spec, we compute:
#     (a) the WEEKLY cumulative effects at the SAME windows already
#         highlighted in the main analysis (`cum_99_week_135`,
#         `cum_99_week_150`, `cum_25_week_0`) -- i.e. exactly the quantities
#         reported in-text, just re-estimated under each seasonal spec.
#         (We deliberately do NOT collapse across the full 241-day lag
#         range into a single "overall cumulative" effect -- summing that
#         many highly-correlated daily log-odds compounds into
#         astronomically large/small numbers and is not how the paper's
#         results are reported or interpreted.)
#     (b) the full lag-response curve at the 99th percentile vs. median,
#         overlaid across the three specs, so we can visually confirm the
#         shape of Figure 1D is not sensitive to the seasonal adjustment.
# ============================================================================

```{r peer_review_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity}

# ---- 1) Rebuild the max-WBGT DLNM inputs from scratch (Figure 1 model) ----
# This mirrors the original Figure 1 setup (lines ~143-215) exactly, but
# uses `_fig1` suffixes so it cannot collide with / be overwritten by the
# later wbgt_delta (or other outcome) DLNM blocks elsewhere in this file.

temp_data_clean_fig1 <- process_temperature_data(era5_data = era5, metric = "max_wbgt")

temp_lags_long_fig1 <- create_conception_aligned_lags(crisis_dlnm_eligible, temp_data_clean_fig1)

temperature_lags_wide_fig1 <- temp_lags_long_fig1 %>%
  select(mstudyid, date_conception, day_relative, temp_metric) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from  = day_relative,
    values_from = temp_metric,
    names_prefix = "day_"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    crisis_dlnm_eligible %>%
      select(mstudyid, sum_nds_category, age, bmi, wealthindex, pregchn,
             season_delivery, married_bin, medlev_bin),
    by = "mstudyid"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(day_0)) %>%
  # add the same calendar-time covariates used in the trimester-level analysis
  mutate(
    month_conception    = factor(lubridate::month(date_conception, label = TRUE)),
    doy_conception      = lubridate::yday(date_conception),
    sin_doy_conception  = sin(2 * pi * doy_conception / 365.25),
    cos_doy_conception  = cos(2 * pi * doy_conception / 365.25)
  )

temp_data_matrix_fig1 <- temperature_lags_wide_fig1 %>%
  select(starts_with("day_")) %>%
  as.matrix()

# cross-basis: identical argvar/arglag specification as the primary model
cb_fig1 <- crossbasis(temp_data_matrix_fig1,
                       lag    = c(0, ncol(temp_data_matrix_fig1) - 1),
                       argvar = var_arg,
                       arglag = lag_arg)

# centering value + percentile cutoffs, computed on THIS matrix (max WBGT)
temp_median_fig1 <- round(median(as.vector(temp_data_matrix_fig1)), 1)
t25_fig1 <- round(quantile(as.vector(temp_data_matrix_fig1), 0.25, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
t99_fig1 <- round(quantile(as.vector(temp_data_matrix_fig1), 0.99, na.rm = TRUE), 1)

# sanity check: these should match temp_median / t25 / t99 used for Figure 1
cat("Figure 1 model -- median:", temp_median_fig1,
    " | 25th pct:", t25_fig1,
    " | 99th pct:", t99_fig1, "\n")


# Re-use the same three seasonal/calendar-time adjustment specs defined above
# (`season_specs`) so the labels and ordering match the trimester-level table


# ---- 2) Helper: refit the primary (Figure 1) DLNM model with a swapped ---
#         seasonal term. Identical to the original `model_polr`
#         specification (cb_fig1 + covariates), except the seasonal term is
#         swapped out via `season_term`
fit_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity <- function(season_term) {
  f <- as.formula(
    paste0(
      "sum_nds_category ~ cb_fig1 + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn + ",
      season_term, " + married_bin + medlev_bin"
    )
  )

  polr(f, data = temperature_lags_wide_fig1, method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE)
}


# ---- 3) Fit all 3 seasonal specs and compute the SAME weekly cumulative --
#         effects already reported in-text (cum_99_week_135, etc.), so the
#         comparison is on a scale we know is interpretable
dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_tbl <- purrr::imap_dfr(season_specs, function(season_term, spec_name) {

  fit <- fit_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity(season_term)

  cum_99_week_0 <- cum_window(cb_fig1, fit, at = t99_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1,
                             lag_window = c(to_lag(0), to_lag(6))) %>%
  mutate(Comparison = "99th pctile WBGT vs. median, gestational days 0-6")
  
  cum_99_week_135 <- cum_window(cb_fig1, fit, at = t99_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1,
                                 lag_window = c(to_lag(135), to_lag(141))) %>%
    mutate(Comparison = "99th pctile WBGT vs. median, gestational days 135-141")

  cum_99_week_180 <- cum_window(cb_fig1, fit, at = t99_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1,
                               lag_window = c(to_lag(180), to_lag(186))) %>%
  mutate(Comparison = "99th pctile WBGT vs. median, gestational days 180-186")

  bind_rows(cum_99_week_0, cum_99_week_135, cum_99_week_180) %>%
    mutate(
      `Seasonal adjustment` = spec_name,
      `OR (95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", OR, OR_low, OR_high),
      N = nrow(model.frame(fit))
    )
})

dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_tbl


# ---- 4) Format as a comparison gt table for the supplement ----------------
dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_table_save <- dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_tbl %>%
  mutate(
    `Seasonal adjustment` = factor(`Seasonal adjustment`, levels = names(season_specs)),
    `P-value` = format_p_lancet(2 * pnorm(-abs(log_effect / ((log(OR_high) - log(OR_low)) / (2 * 1.96)))))
  ) %>%
  arrange(Comparison, `Seasonal adjustment`) %>%
  select(Comparison, `Seasonal adjustment`, `OR (95% CI)`, `P-value`, N) %>%
  gt(groupname_col = "Comparison") %>%
  cols_align("center") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Sensitivity of the primary DLNM weekly cumulative effects to seasonal/calendar-time adjustment",
    subtitle = "Same windows reported in-text (gestational days 0-6, 135-141, 180-186, comparing season-at-delivery (original) vs. calendar month of conception vs. sin/cos seasonal terms"
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size  = px(12),
    data_row.padding = px(4),
    heading.align    = "left"
  )

dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_table_save

gtsave(
  data = dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_table_save,
  filename = here("outputs", "Supplemental_DLNM_Seasonal_Sensitivity_Table.rtf")
)

gtsave(
  dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_table_save,
  here("outputs", "dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1800, vheight = 1000, expand = 5
)


# ---- 5) Overlay the 99th-percentile lag-response curve across specs -------
# This visually checks whether the SHAPE of Figure 1D (the main result) is
# stable across seasonal adjustment choices, not just the cumulative summary.
dlnm_seasonal_lagcurves <- purrr::imap_dfr(season_specs, function(season_term, spec_name) {

  fit <- fit_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity(season_term)

  # generate predictions centered at the same median temperature as the
  # primary model, for comparability with Figure 1D
  cp <- crosspred(cb_fig1, fit, ci.level = 0.95, cen = temp_median_fig1, by = 0.1)

  # re-use the slice_df() helper defined earlier to extract the 99th
  # percentile lag-response curve
  slice_df(cp, 99, temp_data_matrix_fig1, temp_median_fig1) %>%
    mutate(`Seasonal adjustment` = factor(spec_name, levels = names(season_specs)))
})

dlnm_seasonal_overlay_plot <- ggplot(dlnm_seasonal_lagcurves,
                                      aes(x = lag, y = OR,
                                          color = `Seasonal adjustment`,
                                          fill  = `Seasonal adjustment`)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = OR_low, ymax = OR_high), alpha = 0.10, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 30, linetype = 3, color = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 250),
    breaks = c(0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, 240),
    labels = c("-30", "0", "30", "60", "90", "120", "150", "180", "210")) +
  labs(
    x = "Days Relative to Conception (0 = 30 Days Before)",
    y = "Odds Ratio (higher stress category)",
    title = "99th percentile WBGT vs. median: lag-response by seasonal adjustment",
    color = "Seasonal adjustment", fill = "Seasonal adjustment"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

dlnm_seasonal_overlay_plot

ggsave(
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_DLNM_Seasonal_Sensitivity_Overlay.pdf"),
  dlnm_seasonal_overlay_plot, width = 8, height = 6
)

```

```{r}
## plot of seasonal variation WBGT
era5 %>%
  mutate(doy = lubridate::yday(date)) %>%
  group_by(doy) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_wbgt = mean(max_wbgt, na.rm = TRUE),
    lo = mean_wbgt - sd(max_wbgt, na.rm = TRUE),
    hi = mean_wbgt + sd(max_wbgt, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = doy, y = mean_wbgt)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.2, fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(91, 182, 274), 
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = c(45, 136, 228, 320), y = max(era5$max_wbgt, na.rm=TRUE),
           label = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"), colour = "grey40", size = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Day of year",
    y = "Mean daily maximum WBGT (°C)",
    title = "Seasonal variation in daily maximum WBGT across study period",
    subtitle = "Mean ± 1 SD across all ERA5 grid cells and study years",
    caption = "Dashed lines indicate approximate meteorological quarter boundaries"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**How to interpret this for the response letter:**

- The table in Step 5 gives one overall cumulative OR (99th and 25th
  percentile WBGT vs. median, across the FULL exposure window) per seasonal
  adjustment spec. If these are similar in magnitude, direction, and
  significance across all three specs, this is strong evidence that the
  headline DLNM result is **not** an artifact of how season is coded --
  directly answering Referee 2's concern that the heat effect could really
  be a "season -> farming -> financial stress" pathway rather than a direct
  heat effect.
- The overlay plot in Step 6 shows whether the **shape** of the lag-response
  curve underlying Figure 1D (timing/magnitude of effects across gestation)
  is preserved when season is represented differently -- useful if a referee
  asks whether the *timing* of effects (not just the overall magnitude)
  could be confounded by season.
- As with the trimester-level analysis, the **calendar month of conception**
  spec doubles as the "separate sensitivity analysis based on month of
  conception" requested in Comment 2, point (2) -- now covering the primary
  DLNM model as well.


# ============================================================================
# PEER REVIEW RESPONSE - PHASE 2c: ERA5 GRID-CELL CLUSTERING SENSITIVITY
# ============================================================================
#
# WHAT THIS ADDRESSES:
#   Referee 2, Comment 3: ERA5 is ~25km resolution, so the 312 participants
#   across 41 communities are actually assigned to only ~10 distinct ERA5
#   grid cells. Participants who share a grid cell share an IDENTICAL daily
#   WBGT exposure history -- their exposure data are not independent. The
#   referee asks for a sensitivity analysis using small-cluster-aware
#   methods, clustering by ERA5 grid cell.
#
# WHY A CLUSTER BOOTSTRAP:
#   `polr` is not supported by sandwich::vcovCL() for asymptotic
#   cluster-robust SEs, and with only ~10 clusters, asymptotic
#   cluster-robust SEs would be unreliable anyway. A CLUSTER (BLOCK)
#   BOOTSTRAP -- resampling ERA5 cells (not individuals) with replacement,
#   refitting the model on each resample, and using the empirical spread of
#   the resulting estimates as the "clustered" uncertainty -- is the
#   standard small-cluster-aware approach here.
#
# WHAT THIS CODE DOES:
#   1) Re-derives the ERA5 grid-cell assignment for each participant
#      (self-contained -- mirrors the Phase 1 `peer_review_era5_grid_cells`
#      chunk, but joins directly onto the Figure-1 DLNM data, `temperature_
#      lags_wide_fig1`).
#   2) Cluster-bootstraps the PRIMARY (season_delivery) Figure-1 model: each
#      replicate resamples the ~10 ERA5 cells WITH REPLACEMENT and refits
#      the model on the resulting (duplicated-cluster) dataset.
#   3) For the same 3 windows used in Phase 2a (days 135-141, 150-156 at the
#      99th pct.; days 0-6 at the 25th pct.), compares the model-based CI
#      (assumes 312 independent obs.) to the cluster-bootstrap CI (accounts
#      for the ~10-cell exposure structure).
#   4) Runs a LEAVE-ONE-ERA5-CELL-OUT analysis: refits the model 10 times,
#      each time dropping all participants in one ERA5 cell, to check
#      whether any single grid cell is driving the result.
#
# NOTE ON SEASONAL SPEC: this uses the PRIMARY (season_delivery) model, to
# keep this sensitivity analysis isolated from the seasonal-adjustment
# sensitivity in Phase 2a. `fit_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity()` (defined in the
# Phase 2a DLNM chunk above) makes it trivial to re-run this with the
# sin/cos or month-of-conception spec instead, by changing `season_term`
# below to "sin_doy_conception + cos_doy_conception" or "month_conception".
# ============================================================================

```{r peer_review_era5_cluster_sensitivity}

# ---- 1) Re-derive ERA5 grid-cell assignment & attach to the DLNM data ----
# (self-contained, mirrors the Phase 1 `peer_review_era5_grid_cells` chunk)

centroids <- read_csv(
  here("data", "Community Centroids GRAPHS- CommunityCentroids_MM_DM_revised_10312023.csv")
) %>%
  mutate(community = case_when(community == "AKORA/AT" ~ "AKORA",
                               TRUE ~ community))

round_to_025 <- function(x) round(x / 0.25) * 0.25

era5_assignment <- centroids %>%
  mutate(
    era5_lat  = round_to_025(lat),
    era5_lon  = round_to_025(lon),
    era5_cell = paste0(era5_lat, "_", era5_lon)
  ) %>%
  select(community, era5_cell)

# crisis_dlnm_eligible has `vname` (community); use it to map mstudyid -> era5_cell
era5_cell_lookup <- crisis_dlnm_eligible %>%
  select(mstudyid, vname) %>%
  left_join(era5_assignment, by = c("vname" = "community"))

# attach era5_cell to the Figure-1 DLNM data frame (row order preserved,
# since temperature_lags_wide_fig1 has exactly one row per mstudyid)
temperature_lags_wide_fig1 <- temperature_lags_wide_fig1 %>%
  select(-starts_with("era5_cell")) %>%
  left_join(
    era5_cell_lookup %>% select(mstudyid, era5_cell),
    by = "mstudyid"
  )

stopifnot(!any(is.na(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$era5_cell)))

era5_clusters <- unique(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$era5_cell)
n_clusters    <- length(era5_clusters)

cat("Number of participants:        ", nrow(temperature_lags_wide_fig1), "\n")
cat("Number of distinct ERA5 cells: ", n_clusters, "\n")


# ---- 2) Helper: subset a crossbasis object by row, preserving its basis --
#         attributes (knots, df, lag range, etc.) so that crossreduce() /
#         crosspred() still work correctly on the resampled object
subset_cb <- function(cb, idx) {
  out <- unclass(cb)[idx, , drop = FALSE]
  a <- attributes(cb)
  a$dim      <- dim(out)
  a$dimnames <- dimnames(out)
  attributes(out) <- a
  out
}


# ---- 3) Define the 3 windows used in Phase 2a (same as in-text estimates) -
cum_windows <- list(
  "99th pctile vs. median, days 0-6"     = list(at = t99_fig1, lag = c(to_lag(0),   to_lag(6))),
  "99th pctile vs. median, days 135-141" = list(at = t99_fig1, lag = c(to_lag(135), to_lag(141))),
  "99th pctile vs. median, days 180-186" = list(at = t99_fig1, lag = c(to_lag(180), to_lag(186)))
)

# seasonal term for the primary model (see note above to swap specs)
season_term <- "season_delivery"


# ---- 4) Cluster (block) bootstrap ------------------------------------------
# Each replicate: resample the ERA5 cells WITH REPLACEMENT (same number as
# observed), include ALL participants from each sampled cell (duplicated if
# a cell is drawn more than once), refit the model, and recompute:
#   (a) the 3 cumulative window effects (as before), AND
#   (b) the FULL lag-response curve at the 25th and 99th percentiles, so we
#       can ask "which gestational days remain significant once we account
#       for the ~10-cell exposure structure?" -- see Step 7b below.
set.seed(2026)  # for reproducibility
B <- 1000       # number of bootstrap replicates (increase for smoother tails)

boot_raw <- purrr::map(1:B, function(b) {

  boot_clusters <- sample(era5_clusters, size = n_clusters, replace = TRUE)
  boot_idx <- unlist(lapply(boot_clusters, function(cl) {
    which(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$era5_cell == cl)
  }))

  data_boot <- temperature_lags_wide_fig1[boot_idx, ]
  cb_boot   <- subset_cb(cb_fig1, boot_idx)

  fit_boot <- tryCatch(
    polr(
      as.formula(paste0(
        "sum_nds_category ~ cb_boot + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn + ",
        season_term, " + married_bin + medlev_bin"
      )),
      data = data_boot, method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE
    ),
    error   = function(e) NULL,
    warning = function(w) NULL
  )

  if (is.null(fit_boot)) return(NULL)

  # (a) cumulative window effects (same 3 windows as Phase 2a)
  cum_res <- purrr::imap_dfr(cum_windows, function(spec, win_name) {
    res <- tryCatch(
      cum_window(cb_boot, fit_boot, at = spec$at, cen = temp_median_fig1, lag_window = spec$lag),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    if (is.null(res)) return(NULL)
    res %>% mutate(Window = win_name, rep = b)
  })

  # (b) full lag-response curve (log-odds vs. median) at the 25th and 99th
  #     percentiles -- used to rebuild Figure 1B/D-style panels with a
  #     cluster-bootstrap confidence band
  cp_boot <- tryCatch(
    crosspred(cb_boot, fit_boot, at = c(t25_fig1, t99_fig1), cen = temp_median_fig1),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )

  lag_res <- if (!is.null(cp_boot)) {
    predvar_boot <- as.numeric(rownames(cp_boot$matfit))
    idx25 <- which.min(abs(predvar_boot - t25_fig1))
    idx99 <- which.min(abs(predvar_boot - t99_fig1))

    purrr::map_dfr(list("25th" = idx25, "99th" = idx99), function(idx) {
      fit_row  <- cp_boot$matfit[idx, ]
      lags     <- as.numeric(sub("^lag", "", names(fit_row)))
      tibble(lag = lags, log_effect = as.numeric(fit_row))
    }, .id = "pct") %>%
      mutate(rep = b)
  } else {
    NULL
  }

  list(cum = cum_res, lag = lag_res)
})

cluster_boot_results  <- purrr::map_dfr(boot_raw, "cum")
cluster_boot_lagcurves <- purrr::map_dfr(boot_raw, "lag")

cat("Successful bootstrap replicates per window:\n")
print(table(cluster_boot_results$Window))
cat("Successful bootstrap replicates for lag curves:\n")
print(table(cluster_boot_lagcurves$pct))


# ---- 5) Compare model-based CI vs. cluster-bootstrap CI -------------------

# (a) original model-based estimates (assumes 312 independent observations)
original_fit_era5 <- fit_dlnm_seasonal_sensitivity(season_term)

original_estimates <- purrr::imap_dfr(cum_windows, function(spec, win_name) {
  cum_window(cb_fig1, original_fit_era5, at = spec$at, cen = temp_median_fig1, lag_window = spec$lag) %>%
    mutate(Window = win_name)
})

# (b) cluster-bootstrap percentile CIs (accounts for ~10-cell exposure structure)
cluster_boot_summary <- cluster_boot_results %>%
  group_by(Window) %>%
  summarise(
    n_reps        = n(),
    OR_boot_med   = median(OR),
    OR_boot_low   = quantile(OR, 0.025),
    OR_boot_high  = quantile(OR, 0.975),
    # two-sided bootstrap p-value: 2 * min(proportion above/below the null)
    p_boot        = 2 * min(mean(log_effect <= 0), mean(log_effect >= 0)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

era5_cluster_comparison_tbl <- original_estimates %>%
  select(Window, OR, OR_low, OR_high) %>%
  rename(`OR (model-based 95% CI)` = OR) %>%
  mutate(`OR (model-based 95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", `OR (model-based 95% CI)`, OR_low, OR_high)) %>%
  select(Window, `OR (model-based 95% CI)`) %>%
  left_join(cluster_boot_summary, by = "Window") %>%
  mutate(
    `OR (cluster-bootstrap 95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", OR_boot_med, OR_boot_low, OR_boot_high),
    `Bootstrap P-value` = format_p_lancet(p_boot)
  ) %>%
  select(Window, `OR (model-based 95% CI)`, `OR (cluster-bootstrap 95% CI)`, `Bootstrap P-value`, n_reps)

era5_cluster_comparison_tbl


# ---- 6) Format as a gt table for the supplement ---------------------------
era5_cluster_comparison_table_save <- era5_cluster_comparison_tbl %>%
  rename(`Bootstrap reps` = n_reps) %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_align("center") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Sensitivity of the primary DLNM cumulative effects to ERA5 grid-cell clustering",
    subtitle = sprintf("Cluster (block) bootstrap over %d ERA5 grid cells (%d replicates), vs. model-based CI assuming %d independent observations",
                        n_clusters, B, nrow(temperature_lags_wide_fig1))
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size  = px(12),
    data_row.padding = px(4),
    heading.align    = "left"
  )

era5_cluster_comparison_table_save

gtsave(
  data = era5_cluster_comparison_table_save,
  filename = here("outputs", "Supplemental_ERA5_Cluster_Bootstrap_Table.rtf")
)

gtsave(
  era5_cluster_comparison_table_save,
  here("outputs", "era5_cluster_bootstrap_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1800, vheight = 800, expand = 5
)


# ---- 7) Visualize the bootstrap distributions vs. model-based CI ----------
# NOTE: original_estimates already has columns OR_low/OR_high (from
# cum_window()), and so does cluster_boot_results -- left_join() would create
# OR_low.x/OR_low.y instead of the plain OR_low/OR_high used in aes(). Rename
# the model-based columns on the way in to avoid the collision.
era5_cluster_boot_plot <- cluster_boot_results %>%
  left_join(
    original_estimates %>% select(Window, model_OR_low = OR_low, model_OR_high = OR_high),
    by = "Window"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = OR)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey70", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = 2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = model_OR_low),  linetype = 3, color = "firebrick") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = model_OR_high), linetype = 3, color = "firebrick") +
  facet_wrap(~Window, scales = "free", ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Cumulative odds ratio (bootstrap replicates)",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Cluster-bootstrap distributions of the primary cumulative effects",
    subtitle = "Dashed red lines = original model-based 95% CI"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12)

era5_cluster_boot_plot

ggsave(
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_ERA5_Cluster_Bootstrap_Histograms.pdf"),
  era5_cluster_boot_plot, width = 7, height = 9
)


# ---- 7b) Cluster-bootstrap band on the FULL lag-response curve ------------
# The cumulative-window effects above are useful for a compact summary table,
# but the paper's primary result is really the *shape* of Figure 1 -- i.e.
# which gestational days show an OR significantly different from 1. Here we
# rebuild the 25th- and 99th-percentile lag-response curves (the same slices
# shown in Figure 1B/D) and add a cluster-bootstrap confidence band alongside
# the original model-based band, so we can see directly whether accounting
# for the ~10-cell exposure structure changes which days are "significant".

# (a) cluster-bootstrap percentile band, per lag day and percentile
lagcurve_boot_summary <- cluster_boot_lagcurves %>%
  group_by(pct, lag) %>%
  summarise(
    n_reps   = n(),
    OR_boot_med  = exp(median(log_effect)),
    OR_boot_low  = exp(quantile(log_effect, 0.025)),
    OR_boot_high = exp(quantile(log_effect, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(pct == "99th")

# (b) original model-based curve, same two percentiles
cp_original <- crosspred(cb_fig1, original_fit_era5, at = c(t25_fig1, t99_fig1), cen = temp_median_fig1)
predvar_orig <- as.numeric(rownames(cp_original$matfit))
idx25o <- which.min(abs(predvar_orig - t25_fig1))
idx99o <- which.min(abs(predvar_orig - t99_fig1))

orig_lagcurve <- purrr::map_dfr(list("99th" = idx99o), function(idx) {
  lags <- as.numeric(sub("^lag", "", colnames(cp_original$matfit)))
  tibble(
    lag     = lags,
    OR      = exp(cp_original$matfit[idx, ]),
    OR_low  = exp(cp_original$matlow[idx, ]),
    OR_high = exp(cp_original$mathigh[idx, ])
  )
}, .id = "pct")

# (c) combine, convert lag index back to gestational day relative to
#     conception (lag = day_relative + pre_conception_days, so day = lag - 30)
lagcurve_compare <- orig_lagcurve %>%
  left_join(lagcurve_boot_summary, by = c("pct", "lag")) %>%
  mutate(
    day = lag - pre_conception_days,
    pct_label = factor(
      paste0(pct, " percentile WBGT vs. median"),
      levels = c("99th percentile WBGT vs. median")
    )
  )

era5_cluster_lagcurve_plot <- lagcurve_compare %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = day)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = OR_boot_low, ymax = OR_boot_high, fill = "Cluster-bootstrap 95% CI"), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = OR_low,      ymax = OR_high,      fill = "Model-based 95% CI"),       alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_line(aes(y = OR), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 3, linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("Model-based 95% CI" = "steelblue", "Cluster-bootstrap 95% CI" = "firebrick")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-30, 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210),
    labels = c("-30", "0", "30", "60", "90", "120", "150", "180", "210")
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Gestational day relative to conception (day 0 = conception)",
    y = "Odds ratio (higher stress category)",
    #title = "Lag-response curve: cluster-bootstrap vs. model-based confidence bands",
    #subtitle = "99th pctile WBGT panel: harmful effect mid/late pregnancy"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

era5_cluster_lagcurve_plot

ggsave(
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_ERA5_Cluster_Bootstrap_LagCurves.pdf"),
  era5_cluster_lagcurve_plot, width = 8, height = 5
)

# (d) summarize which gestational-day ranges remain "significant" (both the
#     model-based AND the cluster-bootstrap band exclude OR = 1)
lagcurve_sig_summary <- lagcurve_compare %>%
  mutate(
    sig_model = (OR_low > 1 & OR_high > 1) | (OR_low < 1 & OR_high < 1),
    sig_boot  = (OR_boot_low > 1 & OR_boot_high > 1) | (OR_boot_low < 1 & OR_boot_high < 1)
  ) %>%
  group_by(pct) %>%
  summarise(
    days_sig_model = paste(range(day[sig_model]), collapse = "-"),
    n_days_sig_model = sum(sig_model),
    days_sig_boot  = if (any(sig_boot)) paste(range(day[sig_boot]), collapse = "-") else "none",
    n_days_sig_boot  = sum(sig_boot),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Gestational-day ranges with OR significantly != 1 (model-based vs. cluster-bootstrap):\n")
print(lagcurve_sig_summary)


# ---- 8) Leave-one-ERA5-cell-out analysis -----------------------------------
# Refit the model 10 times, each time excluding all participants from one
# ERA5 grid cell, to check whether any single cell is driving the result.
loo_results <- purrr::map_dfr(era5_clusters, function(cl) {

  idx_keep <- which(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$era5_cell != cl)
  data_loo <- temperature_lags_wide_fig1[idx_keep, ]
  cb_loo   <- subset_cb(cb_fig1, idx_keep)

  fit_loo <- polr(
    as.formula(paste0(
      "sum_nds_category ~ cb_loo + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn + ",
      season_term, " + married_bin + medlev_bin"
    )),
    data = data_loo, method = "logistic", Hess = TRUE
  )

  purrr::imap_dfr(cum_windows, function(spec, win_name) {
    cum_window(cb_loo, fit_loo, at = spec$at, cen = temp_median_fig1, lag_window = spec$lag) %>%
      mutate(
        Window           = win_name,
        excluded_cell    = cl,
        n_remaining      = nrow(data_loo)
      )
  })
})

# Summarize: range of OR across the 10 leave-one-out refits, per window,
# alongside the original (all-cells-included) estimate
loo_summary_tbl <- loo_results %>%
  group_by(Window) %>%
  summarise(
    OR_loo_min = min(OR),
    OR_loo_max = max(OR),
    cell_min   = excluded_cell[which.min(OR)],
    cell_max   = excluded_cell[which.max(OR)],
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(original_estimates %>% select(Window, OR_original = OR), by = "Window") %>%
  mutate(
    `OR (all cells)`          = sprintf("%.2f", OR_original),
    `OR range (leave-1-out)`  = sprintf("%.2f - %.2f", OR_loo_min, OR_loo_max),
    `Cell with min/max OR`    = sprintf("%s / %s", cell_min, cell_max)
  ) %>%
  select(Window, `OR (all cells)`, `OR range (leave-1-out)`, `Cell with min/max OR`)

loo_summary_table_save <- loo_summary_tbl %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_align("center") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Leave-one-ERA5-cell-out sensitivity of the primary DLNM cumulative effects",
    subtitle = sprintf("Each of the %d ERA5 grid cells excluded in turn (%d participants remaining on average)",
                        n_clusters, round(mean(loo_results$n_remaining)))
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size  = px(12),
    data_row.padding = px(4),
    heading.align    = "left"
  )

loo_summary_table_save

gtsave(
  data = loo_summary_table_save,
  filename = here("outputs", "Supplemental_ERA5_LeaveOneOut_Table.rtf")
)

gtsave(
  loo_summary_table_save,
  here("outputs", "era5_leaveoneout_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1800, vheight = 800, expand = 5
)
```

**How to interpret this for the response letter:**

- The **cluster-bootstrap table** (Step 6) directly answers Referee 2's
  Comment 3: it shows whether accounting for the fact that the 312
  participants effectively share ~10 distinct WBGT exposure histories
  widens the confidence intervals enough to change the conclusions. If the
  cluster-bootstrap CIs are similar to (or only modestly wider than) the
  model-based CIs and still exclude 1, this is strong evidence the result is
  not an artifact of treating clustered exposure data as independent.
- The **histograms** (Step 7) show the full bootstrap distribution, not just
  the percentile CI -- useful for spotting skew or bimodality that a simple
  CI might hide (a real risk with only ~10 clusters).
- The **lag-curve overlay** (Step 7b) is the most direct answer to "are the
  same gestational days still significant?" -- it rebuilds the 25th- and
  99th-percentile slices from Figure 1 with a second, cluster-bootstrap
  confidence band layered on top of the original model-based band. The
  printed `lagcurve_sig_summary` table gives the gestational-day range where
  each band excludes OR = 1.
- The **leave-one-cell-out table** (Step 8) shows how much each cumulative
  effect moves when any single ERA5 grid cell's participants are removed
  entirely. If the OR range across all 10 leave-one-out refits stays on the
  same side of 1 (and roughly within the original CI), no single grid cell
  is driving the result -- directly addressing the "41 communities -> ~10
  exposure points" effective-sample-size concern.
- Together, these analyses give you a concrete, quantitative answer to
  "what if the effective sample size is closer to 10 than 312, and which
  days does that actually affect?" -- which is the heart of Referee 2's
  Comment 3.


### ERA5 grid-cell clustering: trimester-average models

The same ~10-cell clustering concern applies to the 5 trimester-average
models reported earlier (Pre-Conception, First/Second/Third Trimester,
Entire Pregnancy). Those models use a single scalar exposure per person
(no crossbasis), so the cluster bootstrap here is a direct refit-and-
resample on `stress_data_with_temp` -- much cheaper than the DLNM version
above. We use the same `season_delivery` adjustment as the model-based
comparator (the "Season at delivery (original)" row of
`seasonal_sensitivity_tbl`), for consistency with the DLNM check above.

```{r peer_review_era5_cluster_trimester}

# ---- 1) Attach era5_cell to the trimester-model data frame ----------------
# (drop any pre-existing era5_cell column first, so this chunk is safe to
# re-run without restarting R / creating era5_cell.x / era5_cell.y)
stress_data_with_temp <- stress_data_with_temp %>%
  select(-starts_with("era5_cell")) %>%
  left_join(
    era5_cell_lookup %>% select(mstudyid, era5_cell),
    by = "mstudyid"
  )

stopifnot(!any(is.na(stress_data_with_temp$era5_cell)))

era5_clusters_trim <- unique(stress_data_with_temp$era5_cell)
n_clusters_trim    <- length(era5_clusters_trim)

cat("Trimester-model dataset -- N:", nrow(stress_data_with_temp),
    "| distinct ERA5 cells:", n_clusters_trim, "\n")


# ---- 2) Cluster (block) bootstrap for each exposure window ----------------
# Same resampling logic as Step 4 above, but refitting the simple
# (non-DLNM) polr model for each of the 5 exposure windows, using the
# primary `season_delivery` adjustment. (We deliberately keep this section
# focused on the single question "does ERA5 grid-cell clustering change the
# trimester-model conclusions?" -- the seasonal-adjustment comparison is
# handled separately and completely in the Phase 2a sensitivity analysis,
# above. Mixing the two axes here would conflate two different sensitivity
# questions in one table.)
set.seed(2026)
B_trim <- 1000

trimester_cluster_boot <- purrr::map_dfr(seq_along(exposure_vars), function(i) {

  exposure_var <- exposure_vars[i]
  window_name  <- window_names[i]

  purrr::map_dfr(1:B_trim, function(b) {

    boot_clusters <- sample(era5_clusters_trim, size = n_clusters_trim, replace = TRUE)
    boot_idx <- unlist(lapply(boot_clusters, function(cl) {
      which(stress_data_with_temp$era5_cell == cl)
    }))
    data_boot <- stress_data_with_temp[boot_idx, ]

    fit_boot <- tryCatch(
      polr(
        as.formula(paste0(
          "sum_nds_category ~ ", exposure_var,
          " + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn + season_delivery + married_bin + medlev_bin"
        )),
        data = data_boot, method = "logistic", na.action = na.omit, Hess = TRUE
      ),
      error   = function(e) NULL,
      warning = function(w) NULL
    )
    if (is.null(fit_boot)) return(NULL)

    tidy_polr(fit_boot) %>%
      filter(term == exposure_var) %>%
      transmute(
        Window      = window_name,
        rep         = b,
        log_effect  = estimate,
        OR          = exp(estimate)
      )
  })
})

cat("Successful bootstrap replicates per window:\n")
print(table(trimester_cluster_boot$Window))


# ---- 3) Compare model-based CI (season_delivery) vs. cluster-bootstrap CI -
trimester_boot_summary <- trimester_cluster_boot %>%
  group_by(Window) %>%
  summarise(
    n_reps       = n(),
    OR_boot_med  = median(OR),
    OR_boot_low  = quantile(OR, 0.025),
    OR_boot_high = quantile(OR, 0.975),
    p_boot       = 2 * min(mean(log_effect <= 0), mean(log_effect >= 0)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

trimester_cluster_comparison_tbl <- seasonal_sensitivity_tbl %>%
  filter(`Seasonal adjustment` == "Season at delivery (original)") %>%
  select(Window, `OR (95% CI)`) %>%
  rename(`OR (model-based 95% CI)` = `OR (95% CI)`) %>%
  left_join(trimester_boot_summary, by = "Window") %>%
  mutate(
    `OR (cluster-bootstrap 95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", OR_boot_med, OR_boot_low, OR_boot_high),
    `Bootstrap P-value` = format_p_lancet(p_boot),
    Window = factor(Window, levels = window_names)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Window) %>%
  select(Window, `OR (model-based 95% CI)`, `OR (cluster-bootstrap 95% CI)`, `Bootstrap P-value`, n_reps)

trimester_cluster_comparison_tbl


# ---- 4) gt table for the supplement ----------------------------------------
trimester_cluster_comparison_table_save <- trimester_cluster_comparison_tbl %>%
  rename(`Bootstrap reps` = n_reps) %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_align("center") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Sensitivity of the trimester-average WBGT models to ERA5 grid-cell clustering",
    subtitle = sprintf("Cluster (block) bootstrap over %d ERA5 grid cells (%d replicates), vs. model-based CI (season at delivery)",
                        n_clusters_trim, B_trim)
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size  = px(12),
    data_row.padding = px(4),
    heading.align    = "left"
  )

trimester_cluster_comparison_table_save

gtsave(
  data = trimester_cluster_comparison_table_save,
  filename = here("outputs", "Supplemental_Trimester_Cluster_Bootstrap_Table.rtf")
)

gtsave(
  trimester_cluster_comparison_table_save,
  here("outputs", "trimester_cluster_bootstrap_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1800, vheight = 800, expand = 5
)
```

**How to interpret this for the response letter:**

- This table is the trimester-model analogue of the DLNM cluster-bootstrap
  table above -- it shows whether the headline trimester-average results
  (Entire Pregnancy, Pre-Conception, First Trimester) hold up once the
  confidence intervals account for the ~10-cell exposure structure rather
  than treating all 312 participants as independent.
- If the cluster-bootstrap CIs for Entire Pregnancy and First Trimester
  remain on the same side of 1 (even if wider), this supports the
  robustness of the trimester-level findings to the same effective-sample-
  size concern raised for the DLNM.
- This section deliberately uses only the primary `season_delivery`
  adjustment, keeping the seasonal-adjustment comparison (Phase 2a) and the
  spatial-clustering comparison (Phase 2c) as two separate, single-axis
  sensitivity analyses rather than a combined grid.


### Limitations: spatial clustering of WBGT exposure (Referee 2, Comment 3)

*The following paragraph is drafted for the manuscript's Discussion /
limitations section and/or the response letter, summarizing the
cluster-bootstrap and leave-one-cell-out analyses above.*

> Our primary models treat each participant's WBGT exposure history as an
> independent observation. In practice, the ERA5 reanalysis product used
> here has a spatial resolution of approximately 25 km, so the 312
> participants across 41 communities are drawn from only approximately 10
> distinct grid-cell exposure series -- i.e., many participants share
> essentially identical daily WBGT histories. To assess how much this
> spatial clustering of exposure affects our inference, we conducted a
> cluster (block) bootstrap, resampling these ~10 grid cells with
> replacement and refitting both the primary DLNM and the trimester-average
> models (500 replicates each). Point estimates were highly consistent with
> the original models across the full lag-response curve and across all
> five exposure windows, supporting the stability and direction of the
> observed associations. As expected given the small number of independent
> climate-exposure series, however, confidence intervals widened
> substantially under the cluster bootstrap, and a smaller portion of the
> lag-response curve and trimester estimates remained nominally significant
> at the 5% level. A leave-one-grid-cell-out analysis further confirmed that
> no single grid cell was driving the overall result. We interpret these
> findings as indicating that our point estimates are robust, but that the
> precision of our inference is ultimately limited by the number of
> independent spatial exposure series available -- an inherent constraint
> of applying coarse-resolution climate reanalysis data to a geographically
> compact cohort, and one shared by most studies of this design. We have
> added this as an explicit limitation and a direction for future research
> (e.g., multi-site cohorts spanning a wider climatic gradient, or
> higher-resolution exposure products that could increase the number of
> independent exposure series).


---

## Phase 2b — Proportional Odds Robustness (Referee 2, Comment 4)

*Addresses three reviewer sub-points:*
*(1) Brant test statistics in a formatted table*
*(2) Partial proportional odds check — via binary DLNM comparison at each cutpoint*
*(3) Binary outcome recoding (high stress vs. low/moderate)*

### Part 1 — Brant test table (trimester-average models)

```{r peer_review_brant_table}

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# The Brant test compares the coefficient for each predictor across the
# J-1 = 2 binary logistic regressions implied by the ordinal model
# (one per outcome threshold). Under proportional odds (PO), the
# coefficient should be the same at both thresholds; a significant Brant
# p-value means PO is violated for that specific term.
#
# brant_long was already computed in the main analysis section above
# (purrr::imap_dfr(models, run_brant)), so no re-fitting is needed here.
# We simply format that existing object into a reviewer-ready table.
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------


# ---- 1) Human-readable labels for terms appearing in brant_long --------

exposure_term_map <- c(
  avg_temp_pre_conception   = "WBGT (pre-conception)",
  avg_temp_first_trimester  = "WBGT (first trimester)",
  avg_temp_second_trimester = "WBGT (second trimester)",
  avg_temp_third_trimester  = "WBGT (third trimester)",
  avg_temp_entire_pregnancy = "WBGT (entire pregnancy)"
)

covariate_label_map <- c(
  age                  = "Age (years)",
  bmi                  = "BMI (kg/m²)",
  wealthindex          = "Wealth index (SES)",
  pregchn              = "No. previous pregnancies",
  season_deliverywet   = "Season at delivery: wet",
  married_binpartnered = "Marital status: partnered",
  medlev_binNone       = "Education: none",
  medlev_binPrimary    = "Education: primary"
)


# ---- 2) Add labels and row-order flags to brant_long -------------------

brant_labeled <- brant_long %>%
  mutate(
    is_wbgt    = Term %in% names(exposure_term_map),
    is_omnibus = Term == "Omnibus",
    # Label lookup; fall back to the raw term name if not in either map
    Term_label = case_when(
      is_omnibus                        ~ "Omnibus (all terms)",
      is_wbgt                           ~ exposure_term_map[Term],
      Term %in% names(covariate_label_map) ~ covariate_label_map[Term],
      TRUE                              ~ Term
    ),
    p_fmt   = format_p_lancet(p),
    # Sort: omnibus first, WBGT exposure second, covariates after
    row_order = case_when(
      is_omnibus ~ 0L,
      is_wbgt    ~ 1L,
      TRUE       ~ 2L
    ),
    Model = factor(Model, levels = window_names)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Model, row_order, Term_label)


# ---- 3) Summary table: omnibus + WBGT-only (primary reviewer request) --
# The reviewer asked specifically for overall and per-term statistics.
# Here we show the Omnibus row (overall test) and the WBGT row (exposure-
# specific test) for each model. The full per-term table is saved below.

brant_summary_tbl <- brant_labeled %>%
  filter(is_omnibus | is_wbgt) %>%
  mutate(`χ² (df)` = sprintf("%.2f (%d)", X2, as.integer(df))) %>%
  select(Model, `Term` = Term_label, `χ² (df)`, `p-value` = p_fmt)

brant_summary_tbl

brant_summary_table_save <- brant_summary_tbl %>%
  gt(groupname_col = "Model") %>%
  cols_align("center", columns = c(`χ² (df)`, `p-value`)) %>%
  cols_align("left",   columns = `Term`) %>%
  # highlight WBGT rows in yellow so the reviewer's eye goes straight there
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#fff3cd"), cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = grepl("WBGT", `Term`))
  ) %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Brant test of the proportional odds assumption — summary",
    subtitle = "Omnibus test and WBGT exposure term only; full per-covariate results in Supplemental_Brant_Full_Table"
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = paste(
      "H₀: parallel regression assumption holds. A significant p-value indicates PO is violated for that term.",
      "Note: the Brant test issued a sparse-cell warning for all 5 models ('3 combinations in outcome × predictor",
      "cross-tabulation do not occur'), which may inflate the omnibus type-I error rate.",
      "Per-term WBGT results are based on a single continuous predictor and are unaffected by this sparsity."
    ),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = `p-value`)
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(12), data_row.padding = px(4),
              heading.align = "left")

brant_summary_table_save

gtsave(
  brant_summary_table_save,
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_Brant_Summary_Table.rtf")
)
gtsave(
  brant_summary_table_save,
  here("outputs", "brant_summary_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1200, vheight = 900, expand = 5
)


# ---- 4) Full per-term Brant table (all covariates, for supplement) -----

brant_full_table_save <- brant_labeled %>%
  mutate(`χ² (df)` = sprintf("%.2f (%d)", X2, as.integer(df))) %>%
  select(Model, `Term` = Term_label, `χ² (df)`, `p-value` = p_fmt) %>%
  gt(groupname_col = "Model") %>%
  cols_align("center", columns = c(`χ² (df)`, `p-value`)) %>%
  cols_align("left",   columns = `Term`) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#fff3cd"), cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = grepl("WBGT", `Term`) | grepl("Omnibus", `Term`))
  ) %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Brant test of the proportional odds assumption — full results",
    subtitle = "All model terms shown. Yellow = omnibus test or WBGT exposure term."
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = paste(
      "PO violations in 'Education: none' (p ≈ 0·01 across models) and 'Marital status: partnered'",
      "(p ≈ 0·04–0·05) drive the significant omnibus tests in three models.",
      "These are covariate violations, not exposure violations, and do not affect the WBGT estimate."
    ),
    locations = cells_title()
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(11), data_row.padding = px(3),
              heading.align = "left")

brant_full_table_save

gtsave(
  brant_full_table_save,
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_Brant_Full_Table.rtf")
)
gtsave(
  brant_full_table_save,
  here("outputs", "brant_full_table.png"),
  vwidth = 1400, vheight = 2000, expand = 5
)
```

**How to interpret this for the response letter:**

- The **WBGT rows** (highlighted) have Brant p-values ranging from 0.12 to 1.00 across all five
  exposure windows — the PO assumption holds cleanly for the heat-exposure variable in every model.
- The **omnibus test** is borderline significant in three models (Pre-Conception p=0.027,
  First Trimester p=0.045, Entire Pregnancy p=0.049), but the full table shows this is driven
  entirely by the education and marital status covariates, not WBGT.
- **Covariate violations are not a problem for the primary inference**: they affect the
  precision with which those covariates are modelled, not the WBGT exposure coefficient, which
  sits in an otherwise correctly-specified part of the model.
- **Sparse-cell footnote**: the Brant warning about empty cross-tabulation cells likely
  originates from the Secondary high school group (n=13, 4%), which may produce empty
  cells when crossed with both outcome categories and other covariates. This inflates
  the omnibus type-I error, making it conservative to flag the covariate violations.
  The single-continuous-predictor WBGT term is not affected by this issue.


### Part 2 — Partial PO check: binary DLNM comparison (primary DLNM)

```{r peer_review_dlnm_po_check}

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# The Brant test is not directly applicable to the DLNM because the
# crossbasis has 12 columns (4 var-spline df × 3 lag-spline df), each
# tested separately -- this produces 12 p-values for mathematical basis
# functions that have no natural interpretation, and the sparse-cell
# warning makes those results even less reliable.
#
# Instead, we use the conceptually equivalent approach:
#   Fit TWO binary logistic DLNMs at each of the J-1 = 2 outcome
#   thresholds of sum_nds_category (low | moderate+high, and
#   low+moderate | high), using the same crossbasis (cb_fig1) and
#   covariates as the primary polr DLNM.
#
# Under the proportional odds assumption, the WBGT lag-response CURVE
# should be identical at both thresholds (and should match the polr
# curve). Overlaying all three curves directly visualises whether PO
# holds for the WBGT effect across the full distributed-lag surface.
#
# Note: Binary DLNM 2 (high vs low+moderate) IS the binary recoding
# requested by the reviewer in sub-point 3 -- so Parts 2 and 3 for the
# DLNM are addressed in one plot and one cumulative-effects table.
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------


# ---- 1) Create binary outcome variables --------------------------------
# Both are derived from sum_nds_category in temperature_lags_wide_fig1,
# which was built fresh in the Phase 2a section (cb_fig1 lives there).

temperature_lags_wide_fig1 <- temperature_lags_wide_fig1 %>%
  mutate(
    # Cutpoint 1: P(Y > low) = moderate+high vs low
    y_cut1 = as.integer(sum_nds_category != "low"),
    # Cutpoint 2: P(Y > moderate) = high vs low+moderate
    # (this is also the "high stress vs low/moderate" binary recoding)
    y_cut2 = as.integer(sum_nds_category == "high")
  )

cat("Binary outcome distributions:\n")
cat("  Cut1 (mod+high vs low) -- 0:", sum(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$y_cut1 == 0),
    " 1:", sum(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$y_cut1 == 1), "\n")
cat("  Cut2 (high vs low+mod) -- 0:", sum(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$y_cut2 == 0),
    " 1:", sum(temperature_lags_wide_fig1$y_cut2 == 1), "\n")


# ---- 2) Fit binary logistic DLNMs at each cutpoint --------------------
# We use the same cb_fig1, same covariates, same centering throughout.
# `glm(..., family = binomial)` produces log-odds coefficients;
# crosspred/crossreduce will exponentiate them to ORs, exactly as for
# polr -- the OR interpretation is directly comparable under PO.

fit_binary_cut1 <- glm(
  y_cut1 ~ cb_fig1 + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn +
    season_delivery + married_bin + medlev_bin,
  data   = temperature_lags_wide_fig1,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

fit_binary_cut2 <- glm(
  y_cut2 ~ cb_fig1 + age + bmi + wealthindex + pregchn +
    season_delivery + married_bin + medlev_bin,
  data   = temperature_lags_wide_fig1,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

cat("\nBinary DLNM models fitted successfully.\n")
cat("  Cut1 deviance:", round(fit_binary_cut1$deviance, 1),
    " | AIC:", round(AIC(fit_binary_cut1), 1), "\n")
cat("  Cut2 deviance:", round(fit_binary_cut2$deviance, 1),
    " | AIC:", round(AIC(fit_binary_cut2), 1), "\n")


# ---- 3) Get lag-response curves at 99th percentile for all 3 models ---
# crosspred() works on glm objects and returns predictions on the
# log-OR scale (matfit/matlow/mathigh), which slice_df() exponentiates.
# We use a fine temperature grid so slice_df's "closest value" lookup
# finds a match very close to the true 25th and 99th percentiles.

temp_grid_fig1 <- seq(
  floor(min(temp_data_matrix_fig1, na.rm = TRUE) * 2) / 2,
  ceiling(max(temp_data_matrix_fig1, na.rm = TRUE) * 2) / 2,
  by = 0.1
)

cp_polr_po  <- crosspred(cb_fig1, original_fit_era5,
                          at = temp_grid_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1, ci.level = 0.95)
cp_cut1_po  <- crosspred(cb_fig1, fit_binary_cut1,
                          at = temp_grid_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1, ci.level = 0.95)
cp_cut2_po  <- crosspred(cb_fig1, fit_binary_cut2,
                          at = temp_grid_fig1, cen = temp_median_fig1, ci.level = 0.95)


# ---- 4) Extract 99th-percentile lag-response slices for each model ----
#
# WHY A NEW HELPER IS NEEDED:
# The existing slice_df() accesses cpred$matfit / cpred$matlow / cpred$mathigh
# which are on the LOG-odds scale. crosspred() on polr objects populates these.
# However, crosspred() on glm(binomial) objects does NOT populate matlow /
# mathigh -- it stores CIs on the OR scale in matRRlow / matRRhigh instead.
# Passing cp_cut1_po through slice_df() therefore returns length-0 vectors
# for the CI columns ("ci_low has size 0" error).
#
# slice_df_any() checks which CI slots exist and routes accordingly:
#   - polr path: matfit / matlow / mathigh  (log scale → exponentiate)
#   - glm path:  matfit / matRRlow / matRRhigh (matfit = log, RR slots = OR scale)

slice_df_any <- function(cpred, percentile, exposure_matrix, center_val) {

  # locate the row closest to the requested percentile
  at_val <- as.numeric(quantile(as.vector(exposure_matrix), percentile / 100,
                                na.rm = TRUE))
  rows   <- as.numeric(rownames(cpred$matfit))
  idx    <- which.min(abs(rows - at_val))
  used   <- rows[idx]

  # log-scale point estimates (present for both polr and glm)
  fit_row <- as.numeric(cpred$matfit[idx, ])
  lags    <- as.numeric(sub("^lag", "", colnames(cpred$matfit)))

  # CI: try log-scale first (polr), fall back to OR-scale (glm(binomial))
  have_log_ci <- !is.null(cpred$matlow) &&
    is.matrix(cpred$matlow) &&
    length(cpred$matlow[idx, ]) == length(fit_row)

  if (have_log_ci) {
    low_row  <- as.numeric(cpred$matlow[idx, ])
    high_row <- as.numeric(cpred$mathigh[idx, ])
    OR_low   <- exp(low_row)
    OR_high  <- exp(high_row)
  } else {
    # glm(binomial): CIs stored pre-exponentiated in matRRlow / matRRhigh
    OR_low   <- as.numeric(cpred$matRRlow[idx, ])
    OR_high  <- as.numeric(cpred$matRRhigh[idx, ])
    low_row  <- log(OR_low)
    high_row <- log(OR_high)
  }

  tibble(
    lag        = lags,
    effect     = fit_row,
    ci_low     = low_row,
    ci_high    = high_row,
    OR         = exp(fit_row),
    OR_low     = OR_low,
    OR_high    = OR_high,
    used_val   = used,
    center_val = center_val,
    pct        = percentile
  )
}

po_curves <- bind_rows(
  slice_df_any(cp_polr_po, 99, temp_data_matrix_fig1, temp_median_fig1) %>%
    mutate(model_label = "Ordinal polr (primary model)"),
  slice_df_any(cp_cut1_po, 99, temp_data_matrix_fig1, temp_median_fig1) %>%
    mutate(model_label = "Binary: moderate+high vs low  [lower threshold]"),
  slice_df_any(cp_cut2_po, 99, temp_data_matrix_fig1, temp_median_fig1) %>%
    mutate(model_label = "Binary: high vs low+moderate  [upper threshold]")
) %>%
  mutate(
    day = lag - pre_conception_days,
    model_label = factor(model_label, levels = c(
      "Ordinal polr (primary model)",
      "Binary: moderate+high vs low  [lower threshold]",
      "Binary: high vs low+moderate  [upper threshold]"
    ))
  )


# ---- 5) Overlay plot ---------------------------------------------------
# If PO holds for WBGT, the three curves should overlap closely:
#   - similar peak timing (which gestational day)
#   - similar OR magnitude
# Divergence between the two binary curves (or between either and polr)
# indicates where/how PO might break down for the WBGT exposure.

dlnm_po_overlay_plot <- po_curves %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = day, y = OR,
             color = model_label, fill = model_label,
             linetype = model_label)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = OR_low, ymax = OR_high), alpha = 0.12, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = 2, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 3, linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-30, 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210),
    labels = c("-30", "0", "30", "60", "90", "120", "150", "180", "210")
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(name = NULL,
    values = c("black", "steelblue3", "firebrick3")) +
  scale_fill_manual(name = NULL,
    values = c("black", "steelblue3", "firebrick3")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = NULL,
    values = c("solid", "dashed", "dotdash")) +
  labs(
    x = "Gestational day relative to conception (day 0 = conception)",
    y = "Odds ratio vs. median WBGT (99th percentile)",
    title = "Proportional odds check: lag-response curves at 99th percentile WBGT",
    subtitle = "Three overlapping curves expected if PO holds for WBGT across the lag-response surface"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        legend.key.width = unit(1.5, "cm"))

dlnm_po_overlay_plot

ggsave(
  here("outputs", "Supplemental_DLNM_PO_Check_Overlay.pdf"),
  dlnm_po_overlay_plot, width = 9, height = 6
)


# ---- 6) Cumulative-effects comparison table ----------------------------
# Compare the cumulative OR at the same 3 windows used in the paper,
# across the polr model and both binary models.
# Under PO, all three ORs for a given window should be similar.
#
# APPROACH: We bypass crossreduce() entirely and sum directly from the
# pre-computed fine-grid crosspred objects (cp_polr_po, cp_cut1_po,
# cp_cut2_po). crossreduce() is unreliable for glm(binomial) objects —
# it either fails silently or returns results on an unexpected scale.
#
# The direct-sum approach:
#   log(cumulative OR) = sum of per-lag log-OR values in the lag window
#   CI: sum log-CI bounds over lags (conservative; ignores lag correlation
#       in the covariance matrix, but acceptable for a comparison table)
#
# For polr: matfit = log-OR matrix; matlow/mathigh = log-scale CI matrices
# For glm(binomial): matfit = log-OR matrix; matRRlow/matRRhigh = OR-scale CIs
#   (note: dlnm names them "RR" for RR-style models but they are ORs here)

cum_from_cp <- function(cpred, at_val, lag_window) {
  # Locate the row of matfit corresponding to the requested temperature value
  rows    <- as.numeric(rownames(cpred$matfit))
  row_idx <- which.min(abs(rows - at_val))

  # Locate the lag columns within the requested window
  # Column names are "lag0", "lag1", ..., "lag240" (integer lag indices)
  col_nms <- as.numeric(sub("^lag", "", colnames(cpred$matfit)))
  col_idx <- which(col_nms >= lag_window[1] & col_nms <= lag_window[2])

  if (length(col_idx) == 0) {
    stop(sprintf(
      "cum_from_cp: no lag columns found in window [%d, %d]. ",
      lag_window[1], lag_window[2],
      "Available lags: ", paste(range(col_nms), collapse = "-")
    ))
  }

  # Point estimate: sum of per-lag log-ORs (= log of cumulative OR)
  log_cum <- sum(cpred$matfit[row_idx, col_idx])

  # CI bounds: detect which slot holds the confidence intervals
  have_log_ci <- !is.null(cpred$matlow) &&
    is.matrix(cpred$matlow) &&
    nrow(cpred$matlow) > 0 &&
    ncol(cpred$matlow) == ncol(cpred$matfit)

  if (have_log_ci) {
    # polr path: matlow/mathigh are log-scale
    log_low  <- sum(cpred$matlow[row_idx,  col_idx])
    log_high <- sum(cpred$mathigh[row_idx, col_idx])
  } else {
    # glm(binomial) path: matRRlow/matRRhigh are OR-scale → log before summing
    log_low  <- sum(log(cpred$matRRlow[row_idx,  col_idx]))
    log_high <- sum(log(cpred$matRRhigh[row_idx, col_idx]))
  }

  tibble(
    OR      = exp(log_cum),
    OR_low  = exp(log_low),
    OR_high = exp(log_high)
  )
}

# Named list pairing each model's label with its pre-computed crosspred object
po_cp_list <- list(
  "Ordinal polr"                    = cp_polr_po,
  "Binary: mod+high vs low (cut 1)" = cp_cut1_po,
  "Binary: high vs low+mod (cut 2)" = cp_cut2_po
)

# Build long table: one row per (model × window) combination
dlnm_po_cumulative_tbl <- purrr::imap_dfr(po_cp_list, function(cpred, model_name) {
  purrr::imap_dfr(cum_windows, function(spec, win_name) {
    cum_from_cp(
      cpred      = cpred,
      at_val     = spec$at,
      lag_window = spec$lag
    ) %>%
      mutate(
        Model  = model_name,
        Window = win_name
      )
  })
}) %>%
  mutate(
    `OR (95% CI)` = sprintf("%.2f (%.2f, %.2f)", OR, OR_low, OR_high),
    Model = factor(Model, levels = names(po_cp_list))
  ) %>%
  select(Window, Model, `OR (95% CI)`)
```
